name: Compose & Safety Model
theme: Create emails with an ironclad draft-first safety model.

criteria.description: 'mailctl compose' command exists and runs without error when given required --to and --subject and a body source, returning exit code 0
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to alice@example.com --subject Hello --body World' via CliRunner with mock_osascript returning a draft id
Verify exit code is 0
Verify the mocked osascript was invoked at least once
criteria.threshold: Command succeeds with exit code 0 and at least one mocked osascript call was made

criteria.description: 'mailctl compose' requires --to and --subject; missing either produces a usage error (non-zero exit, no osascript call)
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --subject Hello --body World' (no --to) via CliRunner
Verify exit code is non-zero
Run 'mailctl compose --to alice@example.com --body World' (no --subject) via CliRunner
Verify exit code is non-zero
Verify mock_osascript.calls is empty in both cases (no AppleScript executed for invalid invocations)
criteria.threshold: Missing --to or --subject yields non-zero exit and no osascript call

criteria.description: '--to', '--cc', and '--bcc' are repeatable; multiple recipients are passed through to the AppleScript layer
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --to b@x.com --cc c@x.com --cc d@x.com --bcc e@x.com --subject S --body B' with mock_osascript
Inspect mock_osascript.calls and verify the generated AppleScript references each of a@x.com, b@x.com, c@x.com, d@x.com, and e@x.com
Verify the AppleScript creates 'to recipient', 'cc recipient', and 'bcc recipient' entries (or equivalent Mail.app dictionary keys) for each address
criteria.threshold: All 5 addresses appear in the generated AppleScript with correct recipient-type classification

criteria.description: Body can be supplied via --body <text>, --body-file <path>, or stdin (when stdin is not a TTY); exactly one source is used per invocation
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body "inline text"' and verify the generated AppleScript contains 'inline text'
Write 'file body content' to a temp file, run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body-file <path>' and verify generated AppleScript contains 'file body content'
Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S' with stdin provided as 'piped body' (CliRunner input parameter) and verify generated AppleScript contains 'piped body'
Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body X --body-file <path>' and verify it errors (cannot use two body sources)
criteria.threshold: All three body sources work; supplying more than one is a usage error

criteria.description: When no --body, --body-file, and stdin is a TTY (interactive), composing without a body source produces a clear usage error rather than silently creating an empty draft
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S' with no body input and stdin treated as a TTY (CliRunner with no input)
Verify exit code is non-zero
Verify the error message names 'body', '--body', '--body-file', or 'stdin' to guide the user
criteria.threshold: Missing body source with TTY stdin yields non-zero exit and an actionable error message

criteria.description: '--attach <path>' is repeatable and each attachment path appears in the generated AppleScript
testSteps: Create two temp files (e.g. /tmp/a.txt and /tmp/b.txt) and run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B --attach /tmp/a.txt --attach /tmp/b.txt'
Inspect mock_osascript.calls and verify the generated AppleScript references both paths and uses an 'attachment' / 'make new attachment' construct
Run with '--attach /nonexistent/path' and verify a non-zero exit with an error mentioning the missing file (and no osascript call to create the message)
criteria.threshold: Existing attachments are passed through; nonexistent attachments fail fast before any AppleScript is run

criteria.description: '--from <account>' selects which Mail.app account the draft/message uses; the account name appears in the generated AppleScript
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B --from Work' with mock_osascript
Inspect mock_osascript.calls and verify the generated AppleScript binds the message's sender to the 'Work' account (e.g. references 'account "Work"' or sets 'sender' from that account's email)
Run with '--from NonexistentAccount' (mock returns account list without it) and verify a non-zero exit and an error naming the account
criteria.threshold: Valid --from is woven into the AppleScript; unknown account fails with an actionable error

criteria.description: DEFAULT BEHAVIOUR: 'mailctl compose' WITHOUT '--dangerously-send' creates a draft and never invokes the Mail.app 'send' verb
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B' with mock_osascript (no --dangerously-send)
Inspect every script string in mock_osascript.calls
Assert NONE of the script strings contain the AppleScript 'send' verb (e.g. 'send outgoing message', 'send newMessage', or 'send msg')
Assert at least one script creates a draft (e.g. references 'make new outgoing message' and 'visible:true' / saves to drafts, or otherwise leaves the message unsent)
Verify exit code 0 and stdout/JSON output reports a draft was created
criteria.threshold: Zero scripts contain a 'send' verb when --dangerously-send is absent; a draft-creation script is present

criteria.description: FLAG REQUIRED TO SEND: '--dangerously-send' is the only flag that triggers the send code path; verified by mocked AppleScript inspection (NEVER by a real send)
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B --dangerously-send --yes' with mock_osascript
Inspect mock_osascript.calls and verify the generated AppleScript DOES contain the 'send' verb applied to the new outgoing message
Verify exit code 0 and stdout reports the message was sent (or the appropriate confirmation)

IMPORTANT: this test runs against the mocked osascript fixture only; under no circumstances should the evaluator invoke 'mailctl compose --dangerously-send' against the real Mail.app to verify a real message is delivered
criteria.threshold: With --dangerously-send (and confirmation provided/skipped), the generated AppleScript contains 'send'; this is verified via mock only, never against real Mail.app

criteria.description: EXPLICIT-PER-INVOCATION: '--dangerously-send' must be supplied on every invocation that sends; the codebase contains NO mechanism (env var, config file, alias, default flag) to set --dangerously-send implicitly
testSteps: Grep the entire src/ tree for any of: 'os.environ' / 'getenv' references checking a MAILCTL_*_SEND or DANGEROUSLY_SEND-style variable
Grep for any config-file reading code (e.g. 'configparser', 'tomllib', 'yaml', 'json.load' over a config path) that could set send defaults
Grep for Typer 'envvar=' parameter on the dangerously_send option
Verify NONE of the above exist for the dangerously_send flag — i.e. the only way to set it is the literal CLI argument
Document the search commands used and confirm the 'safety-as-architecture' principle: the absence of these features IS the safety model
criteria.threshold: No env-var, config, or alias mechanism can set dangerously_send; the flag is only settable via explicit CLI argument on each invocation

criteria.description: CONFIRMATION PROMPT: with '--dangerously-send' but WITHOUT '--yes', the user is prompted for confirmation; declining (default 'N' or empty input) aborts WITHOUT invoking 'send'
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B --dangerously-send' with CliRunner input='' (user just presses enter — default should be No)
Verify exit code is non-zero (aborted) OR exit code 0 with output stating the send was cancelled
Inspect mock_osascript.calls and verify NO script contains the 'send' verb
Run again with input='n\n' and verify the same: no 'send' verb in any script
criteria.threshold: Default-to-No confirmation; declining prevents any 'send' verb from reaching the AppleScript layer

criteria.description: CONFIRMATION ACCEPT: with '--dangerously-send' and confirmation 'y' typed at the prompt, the send code path executes (verified via mock)
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B --dangerously-send' with CliRunner input='y\n'
Inspect mock_osascript.calls and verify a script contains the 'send' verb
Verify exit code is 0
AGAIN: verified via mocked osascript only — never against real Mail.app
criteria.threshold: 'y' confirmation followed by --dangerously-send produces a send-bearing script in the mock; no real send is performed

criteria.description: '--yes' SKIPS the confirmation prompt when paired with '--dangerously-send'; without '--dangerously-send', '--yes' alone NEVER causes a send
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B --dangerously-send --yes' with no stdin input — verify exit 0 and a 'send'-bearing script in mock_osascript.calls
Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B --yes' (NO --dangerously-send) — verify exit 0, a draft was created, and NO script contains 'send'
Confirms --yes is purely a confirmation skipper, not a send authoriser
criteria.threshold: --yes only suppresses the prompt; sending still requires --dangerously-send

criteria.description: '--dry-run' prints what WOULD happen and exits without invoking osascript at all (or invokes only read-only osascript such as account lookups) — verified by mock call inspection
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B --dry-run' with mock_osascript and capture stdout
Verify exit code 0, and that stdout describes the recipients, subject, and that a DRAFT would be created
Verify no script in mock_osascript.calls contains 'make new outgoing message' / 'send' verbs (i.e. no compose action was executed)

Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B --dangerously-send --yes --dry-run' and verify stdout describes that a message WOULD BE SENT, exit 0, and no 'send' verb was actually executed in any osascript call
criteria.threshold: --dry-run produces accurate description on stdout and never executes the compose/send AppleScript

criteria.description: ARCHITECTURAL UNIT TEST: a dedicated unit test verifies the safety model holds across a parametrised matrix of bypass attempts, asserting that 'send' never appears in the generated AppleScript unless the literal '--dangerously-send' CLI flag was passed
testSteps: Open tests/unit/test_safety_model.py (or equivalent) and verify it contains a parametrised test that covers, at minimum: bare compose, compose --yes, compose --dry-run, compose with --to/--cc/--bcc, compose with --attach, compose with stdin body, compose with --from
For each parametrisation, the test asserts NO script in mock_osascript.calls contains the 'send' verb
A complementary test runs with --dangerously-send --yes and asserts the 'send' verb DOES appear
All these tests pass
criteria.threshold: A safety-model unit test module exists, covers at least 6 bypass-attempt scenarios, and all assertions pass

criteria.description: 'mailctl compose' (default draft path) prints a human-readable confirmation including the account and indicating a DRAFT was created (not sent)
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B --from Work' with mock_osascript
Capture stdout and verify it contains the word 'Draft' (or 'draft') and the account name 'Work'
Verify the wording does NOT use 'sent' or claim the message was sent
criteria.threshold: Default-path output clearly states a draft was created and identifies the account

criteria.description: 'mailctl compose --json' emits a structured JSON object with at least: action ('draft' or 'sent'), account, to (list), cc (list), bcc (list), subject, and a draft/message id where applicable
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --cc c@x.com --subject S --body B --from Work --json' with mock_osascript returning a draft id
Parse stdout as JSON and verify it is an object
Verify keys present: action (== 'draft'), account ('Work'), to (list containing 'a@x.com'), cc (list containing 'c@x.com'), bcc (list, may be empty), subject ('S'), and an id field
Run with --dangerously-send --yes and verify action == 'sent' in JSON output
criteria.threshold: Valid JSON object emitted with all required fields; action reflects draft vs sent correctly

criteria.description: 'mailctl compose' produces a clear error when Mail.app is not running, mirroring the error pattern from prior sprints
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --to a@x.com --subject S --body B' with mock_osascript configured to simulate Mail.app not running (e.g. set_error('application isn't running'))
Verify exit code is non-zero
Verify error message on stderr mentions Mail.app
criteria.threshold: Non-zero exit and user-friendly Mail.app error message on stderr

criteria.description: Compose command sends data to stdout and errors to stderr, consistent with Sprint 2-4 convention
testSteps: Run a successful 'mailctl compose ...' and verify confirmation/JSON appears on stdout and stderr is empty (or only contains permitted log lines)
Run a failing 'mailctl compose ...' (e.g. missing --to or Mail.app down) and verify the error message appears on stderr
criteria.threshold: Stdout for data, stderr for errors, consistent with prior sprints

criteria.description: 'mailctl compose --help' describes all options including --to, --cc, --bcc, --subject, --body, --body-file, --from, --attach, --dangerously-send, --yes, --dry-run, and --json; the help text for --dangerously-send explicitly warns that the flag SENDS the email
testSteps: Run 'mailctl compose --help' via CliRunner
Verify the help output mentions every option listed above
Verify the --dangerously-send help text contains a warning word like 'send', 'sends', 'real', or 'irreversible' so users understand what the flag does
Verify the help summary makes clear the default behaviour is to create a draft
criteria.threshold: Help enumerates all options and contains a clear warning on --dangerously-send

criteria.description: 'mailctl --help' lists 'compose' as a top-level command alongside the existing 'accounts', 'mailboxes', and 'messages' command groups
testSteps: Run 'mailctl --help' via CliRunner
Verify the output lists 'compose' as a command
Verify 'accounts', 'mailboxes', and 'messages' are still listed (regression check)
criteria.threshold: 'compose' visible at the top level alongside prior command groups

criteria.description: Unit tests exist for compose covering: required arg validation, recipient repetition, all three body sources, attachments, --from account selection, default-draft behaviour, --dangerously-send via mock, confirmation accept/decline, --yes, --dry-run, JSON output, Mail.app down error, and at least one parametrised safety-model test
testSteps: Run pytest for the compose and safety-model test modules

Verify tests exist for each of: required args, recipients, body sources, attachments, --from, default draft, --dangerously-send (mocked), confirmation accept, confirmation decline, --yes, --dry-run, JSON, Mail.app down, safety-model parametrisation
Verify all of these tests pass
criteria.threshold: At least 14 distinct compose/safety-model test cases, all passing

criteria.description: Full test suite (pytest from project root) passes with zero failures, including all Sprint 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 tests
testSteps: Run 'pytest -v' from the project root
Verify all tests pass (0 failures, 0 errors)
Verify Sprint 1, 2, 3, and 4 tests still pass (regression)
criteria.threshold: All tests pass, exit code 0

criteria.description: Compose implementation follows the established architecture: AppleScript generation in build_*_script() functions, parsing where applicable in parse_*_output() functions, thin Typer command handler that delegates to a fetch/perform function and uses the shared render_output() and handle_mail_error() utilities
testSteps: Inspect the compose implementation (e.g. src/mailctl/commands/compose.py)
Verify a build_compose_script() (or similarly named) function constructs the AppleScript
Verify the command handler delegates to a perform_compose() / fetch_* function rather than inlining engine calls
Verify the command handler uses the shared output and error-handling helpers established in earlier sprints
criteria.threshold: Compose follows the build/parse/perform/render architecture pattern from prior sprints

criteria.description: All Sprint 1-4 commands ('mailctl --version', 'mailctl accounts list', 'mailctl mailboxes list', 'mailctl messages list', 'mailctl messages show', 'mailctl messages search') still work with the same options and outputs
testSteps: Run 'mailctl --version' and verify it prints the version and exits 0
Run 'mailctl --help' and verify it lists accounts, mailboxes, messages, and now compose as command groups
Run the full Sprint 1-4 unit test suite and verify all tests still pass
criteria.threshold: All Sprint 1-4 functionality preserved

outOfScope: Draft editing — modifying an existing draft after creation (Sprint 8)
HTML body composition — body is treated as plain text in v1
Inline attachments / cid embeds — only file-path attachments in v1
Verifying real email delivery — explicitly forbidden by CLAUDE.md safety guardrails. The send code path is verified via mocked osascript ONLY.

safetyNotes.criticalConstraint: Per /Users/claude/dev/cli.mail.app/CLAUDE.md, no test in this contract may cause a real email to be delivered. Every criterion that exercises the send code path uses one of: mocked osascript subprocess inspection (C-114, C-117, C-118, C-120), --dry-run assertion (C-119), or default draft-creation assertion (C-113, C-121). The evaluator MUST NOT run 'mailctl compose --dangerously-send' against real Mail.app to verify delivery — that pattern is explicitly forbidden by the project guardrails.
safetyNotes.safeReadOnlyDomain: Sprint 5 introduces no read-only commands beyond what already exists, so the evaluator's safe real-Mail.app testing surface is unchanged from Sprint 4.
safetyNotes.safeWriteDomain: The evaluator MAY, if desired, run 'mailctl compose --to <evaluator's own address> --subject Test --body Test' (NO --dangerously-send) against real Mail.app to verify a draft appears in the Drafts folder, and then delete that draft. This is permitted per CLAUDE.md's safe-by-default testing domain. All other write-path testing must be done via mocks.
